## Merkle trees compress many transaction hashes into one commitment

A block can contain many transactions, but the header still needs a compact way to
commit to the full transaction set. That is the role of the Merkle tree.

A Merkle tree starts from the transaction hashes, then repeatedly hashes pairs of
values upward until only one value remains. That final value is the Merkle root.

In practice, the block header stores the root rather than the full tree.

## Why the Merkle root is useful

The root acts as a commitment to the transaction list.

- If any transaction changes, its leaf hash changes.
- That change propagates upward through the tree.
- The Merkle root therefore changes as well.

This means the header can summarize many transactions with one short hash while still
preserving tamper evidence.

## Why this matters for verification

Merkle trees are not just storage compression. They support efficient proofs of
membership.

Instead of downloading every transaction in a block, a light client can check a much
smaller proof showing how one transaction hash connects to the recorded Merkle root.
That is the basic intuition behind simplified payment verification and other
commitment-based verification workflows.

Students do not need to master every proof format yet. The key point is that a tree
structure makes selective verification possible in a way that a flat list summary
does not.

## What the first coding lab should emphasize

The first Merkle-tree lab does not need production-grade handling of every edge case.
It should make three things visible:

1. transaction hashes become leaf values,
2. repeated pairwise hashing produces one root,
3. changing one transaction changes the root commitment.

That is enough for the rest of the course, where the Merkle root appears as one of
the key fields in a block header.

## What students should be able to report

When students explain a Merkle tree in `TEM 00UU`, they should be able to report:

- what the leaf hashes represent,
- how the tree is reduced to one Merkle root,
- why the root belongs in the block header,
- and why the structure helps light-weight verification.
